page_title: Võ Trọng Đức | Security Engineer & Automation Specialist
hero_description: Building secure systems and automating complex workflows. Passionate about threat detection, algo-trading, and CTF challenges.
about_intro: Security-focused engineer with a passion for building automated solutions and tackling complex cybersecurity challenges. Currently pursuing Information Assurance at FPT University while working on real-world security projects.
about_detail: Experienced in SOC operations, network intrusion detection, and developing automation tools. Active CTF competitor with expertise in forensics, reverse engineering, and web exploitation.
hint_click_card: Click on a card to view detailed information
project_nids_desc: An advanced hybrid NIDS combining Suricata signature-based detection with Machine Learning anomaly detection. Trained on CICIDS2017 with XGBoost achieving 99.46% accuracy. Features 8-step closed-loop detection, behavioral analysis, and real-time Telegram alerting.
project_nids_summary: A state-of-the-art hybrid Network Intrusion Detection System combining Suricata signature-based detection with Machine Learning anomaly detection (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost). Trained on the CICIDS2017 dataset with 99.46% accuracy, featuring real-time alerting via Telegram API and comprehensive behavioral analysis.
project_nids_overview: The system operates through an 8-step closed-loop process to ensure no threats are missed:
project_nids_step1: Baseline Establishment: Normal network traffic data trains the ML model, creating behavioral thresholds.
project_nids_step2: Sniffing: High-speed packet capture connects to SPAN port without network latency.
project_nids_step3: Signature Analysis (SNIDS): Suricata inspects packets against known signature rules.
project_nids_step4: Immediate Alerting: Known attacks trigger instant alerts upon signature match.
project_nids_step5: Forward to ADNIDS: Unmatched packets are forwarded to the ML-based anomaly detection component.
project_nids_step6: ML Analysis: ADNIDS compares packet characteristics against the established baseline.
project_nids_step7: Anomaly Alerting: Significant deviations trigger zero-day attack alerts.
project_nids_step8: System Update: Newly discovered attack patterns are fed back to enhance signature database.
project_nids_detail2: Removed constant/correlated features, mapped to Suricata logs
project_nids_detail4: Suricata eve.json → Feature Extraction → ML Inference
project_nids_arch_intro: The system consists of multiple layers working in harmony:
project_nids_ml_intro: Three complementary ML algorithms form an ensemble for robust detection:
project_nids_alert_intro: Beyond simple anomaly detection, the system provides comprehensive analysis:
project_nids_alert1: Combined Anomaly Score: Weighted aggregation from all 3 ML models for confidence scoring.
project_nids_alert2: Statistical Anomalies: Z-score analysis pinpoints exact features (e.g., total_bwd_packets) deviating from baseline.
project_nids_alert3: Severity Classification: Auto-categorizes alerts (Low/Medium/High) based on model confidence and protocol context (SSH, HTTPS).
project_nids_alert4: Real-time Telegram Notifications: Instant alerts with full attack context for SOC operators.
project_nids_github_link: View the complete source code and documentation
project_nids_tech4: Alerting: Telegram Bot API, File Logging, CSV Export
project_nids_tech5: Dataset: CICIDS2017 (DoS, DDoS, Brute Force, Web Attacks, Infiltration)
writeup_gimme_summary: This forensic investigation reconstructs a multi-stage intrusion chain captured in a PCAP file. The attack leverages CVE-2025-49704 — a critical SharePoint deserialization vulnerability in the ToolShell component — to achieve remote code execution, deploy a credential-stealing implant, and exfiltrate Chrome browser credentials to an attacker-controlled server.
writeup_gimme_h_phase1: 🔍 Phase 1: Network Traffic Acquisition & Initial Triage
writeup_gimme_phase1: The investigation begins with the provided PCAP file. Upon loading it into Wireshark, an initial scan reveals HTTP traffic between the victim host and a remote server at 30.243.67.129. An HTTP GET request returning a start.aspx page is observed — an immediate indicator of a web application-based attack targeting a SharePoint environment.
writeup_gimme_phase1b: Further examination reveals a POST request directed at ToolPane.aspx, carrying a suspicious CompressedDataTable parameter — an anomalous artifact warranting deeper inspection.
writeup_gimme_phase1c: Using File > Export Objects > HTTP, all transferred objects are systematically extracted for analysis:
writeup_gimme_phase1d: After saving all exported files, preliminary triage is performed using strings and file commands. Within the ToolPane.aspx form-urlencoded body, a Base64-encoded CompressedDataTable value is flagged as suspicious.
writeup_gimme_phase2: Searching for the keyword "CompressedDataTable SharePoint" leads to a critical intelligence source: a security advisory published by Viettel Cyber Security (VCS) documenting ToolShell — a severe SharePoint vulnerability chain actively exploited in the wild.
writeup_gimme_phase2_quote: The challenge simulates CVE-2025-49704, wherein an attacker embeds a deserialization payload (XML/serialized .NET object) into the MSOTlPn_DWP parameter of a POST request targeting SharePoint's ToolPane endpoint. Upon deserialization by the server, the payload achieves arbitrary code execution.
writeup_gimme_phase3a: 3.1 — Decompressing the CompressedDataTable: The CompressedDataTable value extracted from ToolPane.aspx is loaded into CyberChef for decoding.
writeup_gimme_phase3b: Applying the standard SharePoint deserialization decoding pipeline — Base64 Decode followed by GZIP Decompress — yields a .NET deserialized XML structure containing a malicious shell invocation.
writeup_gimme_phase3c: 3.2 — Extracting the PowerShell Stager: Within the decompressed payload, the <MethodParameters> node contains another Base64-encoded string. Decoding this in CyberChef reveals the actual attack command — a PowerShell stager responsible for downloading, reconstructing, and executing the implant:
writeup_gimme_phase3d: 3.3 — Attack Chain Reconstruction: The stager operates in a precise sequence designed to evade detection:
writeup_gimme_phase3e: This double certutil decode technique is a well-documented Living-off-the-Land Binary (LOLBin) evasion method — the payload masquerades as a PEM certificate at rest, bypassing content-based detection.
writeup_gimme_phase4a: Following the stager's logic step by step, the final artifact health_check.exe is reconstructed. Upon execution in an isolated environment, the implant reveals its true behavior:
writeup_gimme_phase4b: Observation 1: The implant invokes 7-Zip internally to create a password-protected archive in C:\Windows\Temp\, using a GUID-named directory.
writeup_gimme_phase4c: Observation 2: A folder with GUID {8E2FF114-0F24-4222-85B6-A86144C5157E} is created, alongside its compressed .zip counterpart.
writeup_gimme_phase4d: Observation 3: Inside the GUID folder, two files are staged for exfiltration:
writeup_gimme_phase4e: Verification: Opening chrome_health_result confirms the SQLite magic header (SQLite format 3), consistent with Chrome's Login Data database. The chrome_service_result contains a JSON structure with Chrome's accessibility, autofill, and os_crypt.encrypted_key fields — the AES-256-GCM key used to decrypt credential entries.
writeup_gimme_phase5a: Returning to the PCAP for further evidence, a previously unexamined HTTP POST request to the /upload endpoint on 30.243.67.128:8000 is identified — the exfiltration channel.
writeup_gimme_phase5b: The raw bytes of the uploaded content begin with signature 50 4B 03 04 — the unmistakable magic bytes of a ZIP archive. File entry headers within the archive confirm the presence of chrome_health_result and chrome_service_result.
writeup_gimme_phase5c: The exfiltrated ZIP is extracted from the PCAP, renamed with a .zip extension, and an extraction attempt is made — only to be met with a password prompt.
writeup_gimme_phase6: Recalling that health_check.exe also created a local ZIP archive during execution, a test extraction is attempted. The same password prompt appears — confirming that the implant embeds a hardcoded password used to protect the exfiltrated archive. The ZIP password is baked into the binary itself.
writeup_gimme_h_phase7: 💡 Phase 7: Static Analysis — Password Recovery via Reverse Engineering
writeup_gimme_phase7a: The binary is loaded into IDA Pro for static analysis. A string search for the keyword "pass" reveals three functions of immediate interest: GenPass1, GenPass2, GenPass3 — clearly responsible for constructing the ZIP password at runtime.
writeup_gimme_phase7b: Following the cross-references into the .data segment (IDA View-A), the password fragments are located as UTF-16LE string literals:
writeup_gimme_phase7c: After reconstructing and testing several combinations of the identified fragments, the full password is recovered:
writeup_gimme_phase8a: With the recovered password, the exfiltrated ZIP archive is successfully extracted.
writeup_gimme_phase8b: The chrome_health_result SQLite database is opened and its contents examined. Within the logins table — where Chrome stores saved credentials — a URL entry is discovered:
writeup_for2_phase1: The challenge provides two artifacts: a PCAPNG file containing network traffic from the victim host, and oci.bin — the attacker's backdoor binary. Upon opening the PCAPNG in Wireshark and filtering by protocol, ICMP Echo Request and Reply packets are immediately visible. The payloads are notably larger than standard ping traffic, suggesting embedded data.
writeup_for2_phase2: Examination of individual ICMP packet payloads reveals embedded ASCII command strings. A critical packet contains: download C:\tmp\flag_1s_ck4mpj0n.txt — the attacker is using the ICMP protocol as a covert C2 channel to exfiltrate the target file.
writeup_for2_phase3: Following the packet stream, a TCP packet appears immediately afterwards containing: openfile on remote computers success — confirming the backdoor successfully executed the download command.
writeup_for2_h_phase4: 🔍 Phase 4: Data Exfiltration via Encoded Packets
writeup_for2_phase4: Below the confirmation, a series of subsequent packets carry payloads filled with unusual character sequences — printable characters arranged in patterns not immediately recognizable.
writeup_for2_phase5: All suspicious packet payloads are extracted and concatenated into a text file. The combined output reveals ASCII art — stylized block characters spelling out the flag.
writeup_re02_phase1: The challenge provides a single artifact: RE02.xls — an older Excel binary format with embedded macros. Opening in Protected View reveals a hint: "Tìm đoạn macro chạy calc, bạn sẽ thấy flag" (Find the macro that runs calc, you'll find the flag).
writeup_re02_phase2: Running strings against the binary reveals key artifacts: Flag in J123, the assembly instruction reverse string in B128 + D70 + reverse string in E178, cell fragments {SCV, MY_SUPERPOWER5, and }HCIR_SI_.
writeup_re02_phase3: Initial reconstruction based on strings output:
writeup_re02_phase3b: Assembled result: VCS{MY_SUPERPOWER5_IS_RICH} — but this was rejected. The 5 is a red herring from raw binary extraction, where strings does not respect OLE document boundaries.
writeup_re02_h_phase4: 💡 Phase 4: OLE Macro Analysis — The Breakthrough
writeup_re02_phase4: The specialized tool olevba (oletools suite) properly parses and deobfuscates the XLM macro, revealing the actual cell values:
writeup_re02_phase4b: The critical difference: Cell D70 contains MY_SUPERPOWER — without the 5. Correct reconstruction:
writeup_re02_takeaway1: XLM ≠ VBA: Excel 4.0 macros use a different storage format — specialized tools like olevba are required
writeup_re02_takeaway2: strings can mislead: Raw binary extraction doesn't respect OLE boundaries — the 5 was a binary artifact
writeup_re02_takeaway3: Decoy execution: =EXEC("calc") distracts the analyst from the obfuscated flag
writeup_re02_takeaway4: Cell fragmentation: Splitting and reversing strings across scattered cells is an effective obfuscation technique
project_keylogger_desc: Proof-of-concept malware analysis lab simulating a full attack lifecycle: phishing delivery, credential harvesting, persistence, and C2 exfiltration via Telegram.
project_keylogger_summary: This project is a controlled malware analysis lab designed to simulate the complete lifecycle of an information-stealing trojan. By embedding a keylogger within a seemingly harmless Snake game, it demonstrates how threat actors weaponize legitimate applications to evade detection.
project_keylogger_scenario: A victim receives a phishing email containing an attached 'Snake Game'. Upon execution, the trojanized game operates normally while silently deploying credential-harvesting and persistence modules in the background.
project_keylogger_tech3: Boot or Logon Autostart: Registry Run Keys
project_keylogger_impl3: Script added to Windows Startup folder
project_keylogger_impl5: Chrome SQLite database decryption via AES
project_keylogger_impl6: Data sent to attacker via Telegram Bot API
project_keylogger_phase1: The attack begins with a crafted phishing email containing the trojanized game as an attachment. Social engineering techniques convince the victim to download and execute the application.
project_keylogger_phase2: Upon execution, the Snake game runs normally as a decoy, providing no visible indication of malicious activity. Meanwhile, the embedded keylogger initializes and begins capturing all keyboard input using the pynput library.
project_keylogger_phase3: The malware performs two types of credential theft:
project_keylogger_cred1: Keylogging: All keystrokes are captured and stored locally in a hidden log file.
project_keylogger_cred2: Browser Credential Extraction: The malware accesses Chrome's Login Data SQLite database, decrypts stored passwords using Windows DPAPI, and harvests saved credentials.
project_keylogger_phase4: To maintain access across system reboots, the malware copies itself to the Windows Startup folder, ensuring automatic re-execution on every login.
project_keylogger_phase5: Harvested data is transmitted to the attacker via HTTPS requests to the Telegram Bot API. This technique leverages legitimate cloud infrastructure, making network-based detection challenging.
project_keylogger_detect1: Endpoint Detection: Monitor for processes accessing Chrome's Login Data database or Local State encryption key.
project_keylogger_detect2: Behavioral Analysis: Flag applications making HTTPS requests to api.telegram.org that were not explicitly whitelisted.
project_keylogger_detect3: Persistence Monitoring: Alert on new files created in the Startup folder, especially scripts or executables.
project_keylogger_detect4: Keylogger Detection: EDR solutions can detect SetWindowsHookEx API calls or unusual keyboard hook registrations.
project_keylogger_mitig1: Implement Application Whitelisting to prevent unauthorized executables from running.
project_keylogger_mitig2: Deploy Email Security Gateways with attachment sandboxing.
project_keylogger_mitig3: Enable Windows Credential Guard to protect browser credential stores.
project_keylogger_mitig4: Use Network Segmentation to restrict outbound connections to known C2 domains.
project_keylogger_mitig5: Conduct regular Security Awareness Training for phishing recognition.
project_keylogger_disclaimer: This project was developed strictly for educational and research purposes to demonstrate malware techniques and their detection. All testing was conducted in isolated lab environments. The techniques demonstrated should never be used for illegal or unethical activities. Always obtain proper authorization before conducting security testing.
project_keylogger_video_link: Watch how the keylogger operates inside the Snake game
exp_prospire_desc:
• Architected and deployed Prospire Finance Bot, delivering real-time financial statements for over 1,600 Vietnamese enterprises.
  • Engineered high-performance scraping tools to efficiently analyze Vietnamese stock market data.
  • Developed Prospire Algo-Trading AI V1, an automated cryptocurrency trading system with proprietary strategies achieving $100k volume in the first week.
  • Created automation pipelines to sync crypto price feeds to Google Sheets for real-time performance tracking.
exp_viettel_desc:
• Onboarded customer websites to Cloudrity WAF, fine-tuning security rules to meet specific business requirements.
  • Deployed and configured Cloud WAF (Radware) to mitigate OWASP Top 10 threats (DDoS, SQLi, etc.).
  • Engineered comprehensive security policies: Signature-based, Custom Signatures, Rate Limiting, and API Protection.
  • Conducted security assessments using Burp Suite, ZAP, Kali Linux and performed in-depth log analysis.
  • Implemented DLP (Fortra) policies to prevent unauthorized data exfiltration.
exp_hpt_role: Security Engineer Intern | On Job Training Semester | Grade: 9.2/10
exp_hpt_desc:
• Performed in-depth analysis of MITRE ATT&CK credential access techniques, including network sniffing, unsecured credentials, and input capture.
  • Applied AI/ML models to improve detection accuracy and refine alert tuning for these threats.
  • Developed a proof-of-concept keylogger and data stealer, cleverly embedded in a Snake game, to simulate real-world cyber threats and demonstrate effective defense strategies.
writeup_ezlogic_summary: Reverse engineering a hardware logic system with position-dependent character encoding. Developed brute-force approach to recover flag character by character.
writeup_ezlogic_techniques: Verilog Analysis, Signal Tracing, Waveform Analysis, Character Brute Force
writeup_ezlogic_intro: This challenge involved reverse-engineering a hardware logic system. Analysis of schematic.pdf revealed a complex gate-level implementation requiring signal tracing.
writeup_ezlogic_analysis: Waveform analysis of EzLogic_tb.vcd showed data_out_all changes while success=0. Testing with prefix '0ops{' revealed position-dependent encoding.
writeup_ezlogic_breakthrough: Each character is encoded independently based on position. Developed character-by-character brute-force comparing data_out[i] with data_std[i].
writeup_forensics_summary: Memory forensics investigation revealing fileless malware persistence via Windows Registry Run keys with Base64-encoded credentials.
writeup_forensics_techniques: Memory Analysis, Volatility Framework, Registry Forensics, Base64 Decoding
writeup_forensics_mitre: T1547.001 Registry Run Keys, T1027 Obfuscation, T1552.002 Credentials in Registry
writeup_forensics_intro: Challenge provided Memory.raw dump and password-protected Gift.rar. Objective: extract credentials from memory to unlock archive.
writeup_forensics_analysis: Volatility analysis focused on persistence mechanisms. Registry Run keys revealed suspicious Base64-encoded value.
writeup_forensics_breakthrough: Decoded Base64: 'Password is {Fileless-Malware-Persistence}' enabled Gift.rar extraction.
writeup_unknowns_summary: Audio steganography challenge with flag hidden in frequency spectrum, visible through spectrogram analysis.
writeup_unknowns_techniques: Audio Forensics, Spectrogram Analysis, Frequency-Domain Steganography
writeup_unknowns_intro: WAV audio file requiring forensics analysis to identify hidden data through spectral visualization.
writeup_unknowns_analysis: Sonic Visualiser spectrogram layer revealed text patterns in frequency spectrum.
writeup_unknowns_result: Flag visible directly in spectrogram output.
writeup_tetriffic_summary: USB HID forensics combined with deterministic game analysis. Reconstructed partial QR code from Tetris gameplay.
writeup_tetriffic_techniques: USB Protocol Analysis, HID Keystroke Extraction, QR Reconstruction, Error Correction
writeup_tetriffic_mitre: T1056.001 Keylogging, T1200 Hardware Additions, T1001.002 Steganography
writeup_tetriffic_intro: Challenge combined tetris.pcapng USB capture with tetris.py game featuring predetermined block sequences.
writeup_tetriffic_analysis: Extracted USB HID keystrokes using tshark and custom parser. Replayed inputs in modified game.
writeup_tetriffic_breakthrough: Tetris grid formed partial QR code. Manual reconstruction in Excel + QRazyBox error correction decoded flag.
writeup_st3g0_summary: LSB steganography in PNG image with flag hidden in least significant bits of red channel.
writeup_st3g0_techniques: PNG Analysis, LSB Extraction, zsteg Tool
writeup_st3g0_intro: PNG file requiring LSB steganography analysis to extract hidden data from pixel values.
writeup_verify_summary: File integrity verification using SHA-256 hash comparison across multiple files.
writeup_verify_techniques: Hash Computation, File Integrity, Automated Verification
writeup_verify_intro: Given directory of files and target SHA-256 hash. Identify matching file and decrypt.
writeup_verify_analysis: Automated script computed SHA-256 for all files, identified match: 8eee7195.
writeup_moonwalk_summary: SSTV signal decoding from WAV audio file—technique used in Apollo lunar missions.
writeup_moonwalk_techniques: SSTV Protocol, Audio Signal Analysis, Radio Imaging
writeup_moonwalk_mitre: T1027 Obfuscation, T1001.003 Steganography, T1048 Alternative Protocol
writeup_moonwalk_intro: WAV exhibiting analog signal characteristics suggesting SSTV transmission encoding.
writeup_moonwalk_analysis: Research identified Slow Scan Television encoding—method for transmitting images via audio.
writeup_moonwalk_result: SSTV decoder converted audio to image revealing: picoCTF{beep_boop_im_in_space}
writeup_gimme_summary: SharePoint CVE-2025-49704 exploitation simulating credential theft attack with Chrome data exfiltration.
writeup_gimme_techniques: Deserialization Attack, PowerShell Payload, Chrome Credential Theft, IDA Analysis
writeup_gimme_mitre: T1190 Exploit Application, T1059.001 PowerShell, T1555.003 Browser Credentials, T1560.001 Encrypted Archive
writeup_gimme_intro: PCAP analysis revealing SharePoint ToolShell exploitation with CompressedDataTable deserialization payload.
writeup_gimme_analysis: Decoded Base64+GZIP payload revealed PowerShell downloading health_check.exe. Malware extracts Chrome Login Data into password-protected ZIP.
writeup_gimme_detection: Monitor SharePoint for anomalous CompressedDataTable parameters. Alert on certutil decode chains. Detect Chrome database access.
writeup_for2_summary: Covert C2 communication via ICMP protocol. Attacker exfiltrated data using ping packets with ASCII art encoding.
writeup_for2_techniques: ICMP Tunneling, Packet Analysis, ASCII Art Extraction, C2 Detection
writeup_for2_mitre: T1095 Non-App Layer Protocol, T1048.003 Alternative Protocol, T1041 C2 Exfiltration
writeup_for2_intro: PCAP containing ICMP backdoor traffic with command execution: 'download C:\tmp\flag_1s_ck4mpj0n.txt'
writeup_for2_analysis: ICMP packets contained ASCII commands. TCP stream confirmed 'openfile on remote computers success'.
writeup_for2_breakthrough: Subsequent packets contained ASCII art spelling the flag in stylized text.
writeup_for2_detection: Monitor unusual ICMP sizes (>100 bytes). Alert on printable ASCII in ICMP payloads. Detect command strings.
writeup_re02_summary: XLM (Excel 4.0) macro analysis revealing obfuscated flag through multi-cell string manipulation.
writeup_re02_techniques: XLM Macro Analysis, olevba Deobfuscation, String Reversal
writeup_re02_mitre: T1059.005 Visual Basic, T1204.002 Malicious File, T1027 Obfuscation, T1140 Deobfuscation
writeup_re02_intro: Excel document with XLM macro launching calc.exe as decoy while hiding flag across cells B128, D70, E178.
writeup_re02_analysis: strings output showed 'reverse string in B128 + D70 + reverse string in E178' but contained false character.
writeup_re02_breakthrough: olevba properly deobfuscated macro showing actual cell values without erroneous '5' character.
writeup_re02_detection: Alert on XLM macro sheets. Monitor EXEC function calls. Detect Excel spawning child processes.
contact_description: Open to security engineering roles, research collaborations, and CTF team invitations. Feel free to reach out.
contact_cta_text: Whether it's securing infrastructure, building automation pipelines, or competing in CTFs - I'm always up for a challenge.
project_trading_desc: High-frequency cryptocurrency trading engine with custom signal algorithms, real-time market data pipelines, and automated trade execution across multiple exchanges.
project_bot_desc: Production Telegram bot serving real-time financial statements for 1,600+ Vietnamese listed companies. Built with producer-consumer architecture and async web scraping for high-throughput data delivery to investors.
ctf_gimme_desc: SharePoint CVE-2025-49704 exploitation chain: deserialization RCE to Chrome credential theft via IDA reverse engineering
ctf_for2_desc: Covert C2 communication via ICMP protocol with command execution and data exfiltration through ping packets
ctf_re02_desc: Obfuscated XLM 4.0 macro reverse engineering with olevba deobfuscation and cross-cell string reconstruction
ctf_ezlogic_desc: Hardware logic gate analysis with Verilog testbench modification and position-based brute-force decryption
ctf_forensics_desc: Windows memory dump analysis with Volatility to identify fileless malware persistence via Registry Run keys
ctf_unknowns_desc: Audio steganography via spectrogram analysis using Sonic Visualiser to reveal frequency-hidden text
ctf_tetriffic_desc: USB HID keystroke extraction from PCAP driving a Tetris game that renders a QR code containing the flag
ctf_st3g0_desc: Least Significant Bit steganography extraction from PNG using zsteg bit-plane analysis
ctf_verify_desc: SHA-256 file integrity verification to identify the correct file among thousands of decoys
ctf_moonwalk_desc: Slow Scan Television (SSTV) signal decoding from audio to reconstruct Apollo-era transmitted images
